title: Mixing it up at Monticello
summary: "...[N]ow France, and again unlike Germany, has become an important, if not the most important security partner of the United States, leading the intervention in Libya; undertaking unilateral and appreciated actions in Mali and the Central African Republic; and standing at the ready for the bombing of Syria which Barack Obama succeeded in calling off."
body: A state dinner with scores of glitterati, a visit to Monticello, a stopover in Silicon Valley—home to a French diaspora of 40,000—all this amounting to a dazzling reaffirmation of the French-American relationship. Even when asked at a press conference which he preferred, Britain or France, President Obama neatly sidestepped the question by saying he had two lovely daughters and loved them both and then multilateralized things by saying that that's the way he felt about all our European allies. Even the question of spying on each other was minimized. As President François Hollande stated, "President Obama and myself clarified things…Mutual trust has been restored." ( New York Times , February 17, 2014). Why is the French reaction much less vociferous than that of the Germans to the revelations of NSA spying on U.S. allies? Apart from Germany's 20th century history, I would cite two factors. First, unlike Germany, there has been no U.S. troop presence in France since 1966, when General de Gaulle decided to leave the integrated command of NATO. (France rejoined the command under President Nicolas Sarkozy in 2009.) Secondly, unlike the Germans, the French themselves have spied on the United States in the past, most egregiously in rifling through the suitcases of U.S. officials and businessmen stopping in French hotels. But now France, and again unlike Germany, has become an important, if not the most important security partner of the United States, leading the intervention in Libya; undertaking unilateral and appreciated actions in Mali and the Central African Republic; and standing at the ready for the bombing of Syria which Barack Obama succeeded in calling off. So the exceptional reception of President Hollande was an expression of gratitude for France's new role, at a time when both Presidents are in trouble at the polls. But I apprehend a sub-text to the visit to Monticello, to the home of the most pro-French President of the United States, Thomas Jefferson, who once famously remarked that of course the United States was his favorite country, but France was next. Also, part of the sub-text that I perceive is the fact that the most famous racially-mixed relationships in the history of the United States have been that of Thomas Jefferson and Sally Hemings—and that of Barack Obama Sr. and Ann Dunham. Statements and views expressed in this commentary are solely those of the author and do not imply endorsement by Harvard University, the Harvard Kennedy School, or the Belfer Center for Science and International Affairs. The latest from Belfer See more Recommended citation Cogan, Charles. "Mixing it up at Monticello." Belfer Center for Science and International Affairs, Harvard Kennedy School, February 20, 2014. result.main_content_en: The article analyzes the 2014 state visit of French President François Hollande to the United States, which featured a lavish reception, a visit to Monticello, and a stop in Silicon Valley, symbolizing a strong reaffirmation of Franco-American ties. It contrasts the muted French reaction to NSA spying revelations with Germany's more vocal response, attributing this to the absence of U.S. troops in France since 1966 and France's own history of espionage against the U.S. The author argues that France has become a crucial U.S. security partner, evidenced by leadership in interventions in Libya, Mali, and the Central African Republic. The exceptional hospitality is interpreted as gratitude for this partnership amid domestic political challenges for both leaders. The visit to Monticello, home of pro-French President Thomas Jefferson, and the allusion to historically significant mixed-race relationships (Jefferson-Hemings, Obama's parents) are noted as symbolic subtexts.
result.main_content_zh: 本文分析了2014年法国总统弗朗索瓦·奥朗德对美国的国事访问，此次访问包括盛大国宴、参观蒙蒂塞洛（Monticello）以及造访硅谷，象征着法美关系的强力重申。文章对比了法国对NSA间谍门事件相对温和的反应与德国更强烈的回应，并将此归因于自1966年戴高乐将军决定退出北约一体化司令部后美国军队在法国的缺席，以及法国自身曾有对美国进行间谍活动的历史。作者指出，法国已成为美国至关重要的安全伙伴，这体现在其在利比亚、马里和中非共和国的干预行动中的领导作用。此次超规格的接待被解读为对法国这一伙伴关系的感谢，同时也正值两国总统在国内面临政治困境之际。文章进一步指出，参观亲法的美国总统托马斯·杰斐逊的故居蒙蒂塞洛，以及提及美国历史上著名的跨种族关系（杰斐逊与萨莉·海明斯、奥巴马总统的父母），构成了此次访问的象征性潜台词。
